name: Entresierras Integral Circular Trail
name_original: Pueblos cercanos Río Alagón — circular desde Valverde de Valdelacasa
description: This moderate 32.9-mile (52.9 km) road cycling route near Valverde de Valdelacasa offers scenic views and a good workout.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Valverde De Valdelacasa traverse the diverse landscapes of the Sierra de Béjar comarca in Salamanca, Spain. The region features picturesque mountainous terrain, forests of oak and ash trees, and expansive green meadows. Cyclists encounter varied topography, including the historic Vía de la Plata and sections of the EuroVelo 1 route, offering a mix of challenging climbs and scenic stretches. The area's natural environment provides a suitable setting for road cycling exploration. Best road cycling routes around Valverde De Valdelacasa The most popular road cycling route is Entresierras Integral Circular Trail, a 32.9 miles (52.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 37 minutes to complete. This route offers a comprehensive exploration of the local terrain, including significant elevation changes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Molinos de Pichón – Ascent to Puerto de Cristóbal loop from La Calzada de Béjar, a moderate 16.5 miles (26.5 km) path. This route includes an ascent towards Puerto de Cristóbal, providing views of the surrounding Sierra de Béjar hills. Local road cyclists also love the Vallejera de Riofrío – Candelario Village loop from Fuentes de Béjar, a 31.3 miles (50.5 km) trail leading through varied landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 38 minutes. Road cycling around Valverde De Valdelacasa is defined by mountainous terrain, oak and ash forests, and the historical Vía de la Plata. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various skill levels. The routes in Valverde De Valdelacasa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 7 reviews. More than 1000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Valverde De Valdelacasa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Valverde De Valdelacasa?
A: The region offers a diverse landscape for road cycling, characterized by picturesque mountainous terrain within the Sierra de Béjar comarca. You'll encounter a mix of challenging climbs and scenic stretches through oak and ash forests, expansive green meadows, and along historical paths like the Vía de la Plata. The routes often feature significant elevation changes, providing a varied experience.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for an easier ride?
A: Yes, while the area is known for its mountainous terrain, there are options for various skill levels. Out of 20 available routes, at least one is classified as easy, and 14 are moderate, offering less strenuous options for those new to road cycling or seeking a more relaxed pace. These routes still allow you to enjoy the natural beauty without excessive difficulty.

Q: What are some of the most scenic or historically significant routes to explore?
A: Many routes in the area offer both natural beauty and historical significance. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025732448/entresierras-integral-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entresierras Integral Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; provides a comprehensive exploration of the local terrain. You can also experience the historic Vía de la Plata, an ancient Roman route, which is integrated into several cycling paths, including a newly renovated asphalt section of the EuroVelo 1 route from Los Santos to Endrinal. These routes wind through pastures and offer a rich historical context.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Valverde De Valdelacasa are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and finish at the same point. For instance, the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025732448/entresierras-integral-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entresierras Integral Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a great example of a route that brings you back to your starting point after exploring the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of points of interest or landmarks can I expect to see along the routes?
A: The routes often pass by or near several interesting landmarks. You might encounter the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1690677" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Granite Theme Park&lt;/a&gt;, which pays homage to the local quarrying tradition with monumental sculptures. The historic Vía de la Plata itself is lined with Roman milestones. Additionally, the town of Valverde de Valdelacasa features traditional stone houses and the attractive Baroque-style Iglesia de Santiago. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38281316" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guijuelo Jamón District – Granite Theme Park loop from La Jara&lt;/a&gt; is one route that includes the Granite Theme Park.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Valverde De Valdelacasa?
A: Given the mountainous terrain and the presence of oak and ash forests, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling. Temperatures are milder, and the natural scenery is particularly vibrant. Summer can be warm, especially at lower elevations, while winter might bring colder temperatures and potentially more challenging conditions in the higher parts of the Sierra de Béjar.

Q: Are there any challenging routes for experienced road cyclists seeking significant elevation gain?
A: Absolutely. The Sierra de Béjar region provides ample opportunities for challenging rides. Out of 20 routes, 5 are classified as difficult, featuring substantial elevation gains. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40335365" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vallejera de Riofrío – Candelario Village loop from Fuentes de Béjar&lt;/a&gt; includes nearly 900 meters of ascent, offering a demanding yet rewarding experience for seasoned cyclists.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Valverde De Valdelacasa?
A: The road cycling routes around Valverde De Valdelacasa are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an impressive average rating of 5.0 stars from over 7 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the scenic beauty of the Sierra de Béjar, and the historical elements like the Vía de la Plata, making for a truly engaging experience.

Q: Can I find accommodation or places to eat along the road cycling routes?
A: While specific cafes or pubs on every route are not detailed, the region, including Valverde de Valdelacasa itself and nearby towns like Béjar, offers traditional architecture and local amenities. You can expect to find places to rest and refuel, particularly in the villages and towns that the routes pass through. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3802166" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albergue Alba Soraya&lt;/a&gt; is one example of a hut or hostel in the vicinity.

Q: Are there options for parking or public transport access to the road cycling routes?
A: Information on specific parking areas for each route is not detailed, but generally, you can find parking in the towns and villages where routes typically start or pass through, such as Valverde de Valdelacasa or La Calzada de Béjar. For public transport, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6255607" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Béjar Station and Vía de la Plata Tunnel&lt;/a&gt; indicates historical transport links, and while direct public transport access to every trailhead might vary, larger towns in the Sierra de Béjar comarca usually have bus services.

Q: How long do the road cycling routes typically take to complete?
A: The duration of the routes varies significantly depending on their length and difficulty. For example, a moderate route like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38203177" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Molinos de Pichón – Ascent to Puerto de Cristóbal loop from La Calzada de Béjar&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 26.5 km (16.5 miles), typically takes around 1 hour and 15 minutes. Longer, more challenging routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20136371" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventorro Pelayo Viewpoint – Puerto de Vallejera loop from Altair. Centro de Turismo Rural&lt;/a&gt; (51.4 km / 31.9 miles), can take over 2 hours and 30 minutes.
tips: Near the town of San Esteban de la Sierra is this viewpoint, on the road that goes to San Miguel de Valero. There are good views of the town and the Quilamas Natural Area.

In the southeastern fringe of the province of Salamanca, the municipal complex of San Miguel de Valero is located, in the middle of the Sierra Quilamas, some 60 kilometers away from the charra capital.
At an average altitude of about 950 meters above sea level, the climate is usually characterized by low temperatures and long winters. However, during the summer the days are mild and the nights are cool.

It is the only granite theme park in Spain, created to pay homage to the quarrying tradition of the municipality. It occupies a former quarry transformed into an artistic and natural space.
Monumental sculptures made from granite blocks by the Salamanca artist Amable Diego.
Recreations that evoke menhirs, dolmens, and megalithic structures, including the famous "Salamanca Stonehenge."
Explanatory panels on the history of quarrying, from Roman roads to modern industry.
Beautiful road that goes from Valdelacasa to Valverde de Valdelacasa, two towns located a few kilometers from each other. Wide and green forest landscapes are seen in rainy seasons.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this racebike route?
faqs.answer: The 'Nearby villages Rio Alagon — circular from Valverde de Valdelacasa' route is estimated to take approximately 2 hours and 37 minutes to complete for an average racebike cyclist. This duration can vary based on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: This is a moderate road cycling route, meaning the surfaces are primarily well-paved. While there is an elevation gain of about 770 meters, it's not overly steep or technically demanding, making it suitable for cyclists with reasonable experience and stamina.

faqs.question: Is this racebike route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its 'moderate' difficulty rating and 52.9-kilometer distance with significant elevation gain, this route requires good fitness. It might be challenging for absolute beginners but is accessible for cyclists with a reasonable level of experience and stamina.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or scenic views along the route?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you can enjoy several notable spots. Keep an eye out for the San Esteban Viewpoint and Sierra de Béjar, the charming settlement of San Miguel de Valero, and the unique Granite Theme Park. There's also a section known as From Valverde to Valdelacasa which is a dedicated cycle way.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route in Valverde de Valdelacasa?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas are not detailed, Valverde de Valdelacasa serves as the starting point for this circular route. You should be able to find suitable parking within the village itself to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region around Rio Alagon and Valverde de Valdelacasa, being in Spain, generally offers pleasant cycling conditions outside of the peak summer heat. Spring and autumn are typically ideal, providing milder temperatures and comfortable riding weather.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle in Espacio Natural de las Quilamas?
faqs.answer: This route passes through the Espacio Natural de las Quilamas. Generally, natural parks in Spain do not require specific permits or entrance fees for cycling on established routes. However, always respect local regulations and signage regarding access and conservation.

faqs.question: Is this racebike route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a road cycling route, it is generally not recommended for dogs to accompany cyclists, especially given the distance and potential for traffic. It's best to leave your furry friends at home for this particular ride.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or resupply along the route?
faqs.answer: The route is named 'Nearby villages Rio Alagon', suggesting it passes through or near several villages. Valverde de Valdelacasa, as the starting point, will have amenities. Other villages along the way, such as San Miguel de Valero, may offer cafes or small shops for refreshments and resupply.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with the Mtb Vía de la Plata, the Vía de la Plata hiking - 02 Merida to Salamanca, and the Ruta de los lagares rupestres, among others.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail or out-and-back?
faqs.answer: This is a circular route, meaning it is a loop trail. You will start and finish in Valverde de Valdelacasa, making it convenient for parking and planning your ride.
